videos.title: What is a Bitcoin Halving? Explained by CoinGecko
videos.text: Every four years, the block reward for Bitcoin miners is cut in half in an event called a "Bitcoin halving", and this will continue until all 21 million Bitcoin is issued. But how does the halving work and what exactly does it mean for Bitcoin? And does this mean Bitcoin will cease to exist after all coins are mined, or are there ways to keep Bitcoin up and running for many more years to come? Let’s explore these questions, and much more, in today’s video about Bitcoin Halving.So what exactly is Bitcoin Halving? Bitcoin halving is a process that regulates the amount of Bitcoin rewards issued to miners for helping secure the network. Specifically, it does this by cutting the reward in half roughly every 4 years.. But first, let’s back up a bit. So, as mentioned, the total supply of Bitcoin is capped at 21 million coins. After 21 million coins are mined, no more Bitcoin will be created Since bitcoin is not run by any one single entity, it requires miners around the world to participate in validating transactions and securing the network. In return, miners are rewarded with Bitcoin for every block (which are what transactions are grouped into) they add to the blockchain. At the time Bitcoin was launched, the reward per block was 50 BTC. The code is set so that miners' rewards are cut in half for every 210,000 blocks added to the blockchain. As each block takes roughly ~10 minutes to mine, this means it takes roughly four years to add that many blocks to the blockchain, with the first Bitcoin halving taking place in 2012, halving the reward to 25 BTC, the second in 2016, halving the rewards to 12.5 BTC, and the latest, halving event taking place in May 2020, halving the rewards to 6.25 BTC. The next one is expected to take place in 2024. What does the halving mean for Bitcoin? Based on the basic principles of supply and demand, the Bitcoin halving tends to result in increased bitcoin prices if demand increases or remains constant. This is because the halving reduces the amount of new bitcoin entering the market. Bitcoin’s limited supply of 21 million makes it a scarce and thus valuable asset if the demand for it continues to increase. Estimates predict that the last halving will happen in 2140, after which miners will get zero block rewards for mining, but will still be able to collect transaction fees for keeping the blockchain running. And ideally, the transaction fees will be enough incentive for miners to keep securing the network. So, do you think Bitcoin halving is sustainable in the future?

videos.title: How Bitcoin Prices Are Affected By The 'Halving' | Business Insider Explains | Insider News
videos.text: A major event is going to shake the crypto world. It's called the halving, and it happens once roughly every four years. This will lower the number of new bitcoin entering the market by half. But how does this work, and what does it mean for prices? I'm Laila Maidan, and I cover all things investing and money. So the way that bitcoin works is it's a type of cryptocurrency that operates on a blockchain, which is a decentralized network where transactions can happen peer to peer or person to person. Not one government or entity can have control over it. So because bitcoin is a decentralized network, there are thousands of computers, which is what we call nodes, operating across the globe. And anyone can own and operate one. And what they simply do is they solve a series of complex mathematical puzzles and verify transactions in the network. Now, the node or series of nodes that find the new block are given a reward in the form of bitcoins. Right now, that reward is 6.25 bitcoin. However, that reward is set to be cut in half in April, down to 3.125 bitcoins. These rewards are cut in half every four years, and this event is called the halving. The reason why they're cut in half every four years is, well, because we have a limited supply of bitcoin. There will only ever be 21 million bitcoin in circulation. Now, I know it sounds like we might be almost towards the end, since we've already mined more than 19 million bitcoin. However, the last bitcoin is expected to be mined sometime around the year 2140. Previous halving events have impacted prices substantially. In 2017, we saw the price rally by over 1,000%. The same thing happened in 2021. Now, investors and traders that understand this pattern, what they'll do is they'll add bitcoin to their position during a quiet time in the market, or what's called during the bear market, and they'll hold on to this asset until the halving event. And once that halving event takes place, many that have been holding their bitcoin for a while tend to wait and try to sell at these high prices. When the price of bitcoin rallies and goes up, it tends to pull the entire crypto market up with it. Those who profited off of the price of bitcoin are now taking their profits and throwing it into higher and more riskier bets like altcoins to try and make even more money. So there's a lot of things that can impact the price of bitcoin outside of the halving. One of them is macroeconomics, specifically the US economy and interest rates. A lot of economists and the markets are speculating that the Federal Reserve could begin to lower interest rates by the end or the second half of this year. That could help push the price of bitcoin up. However, it's highly speculative. Earlier this year, in January, the SEC approved a series of bitcoin ETFs. This has made the asset much more accessible, because you don't have to be a crypto expert or a bitcoin expert to know where to buy bitcoin. So now it's more accessible. What does this mean? Well, there's more demand for it. Even though we have historical data about how bitcoin's price could move based on certain events, past performance never guarantees future results. The cons of this asset is, well, it's highly volatile in price, and because of that volatility, it makes it very difficult for it to be a medium of exchange, for example, to be used as money. It also makes it a little scary to hold it as an investor. How price may move for bitcoin in the future still remains unknown. We can say bitcoin is in price discovery mode right now.
